author-notes.corresp: Correspondence to: James R. McKay, Ph.D., University of Pennsylvania, 3535 Market St., Suite 500, Philadelphia, PA. 19104, Tel: +1 215 746 7704, james.mckay@pennmedicine.upenn.edu

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

sec.title: Purpose of review
sec.p: To provide an update of studies on the effectiveness of digital and telephonic approaches to providing remote continuing care for substance use disorders.

sec.title: Recent findings
sec.p: Effective continuing care can be provided via smartphone apps, text messaging, interactive voice response, and structured telephone counseling. The remote continuing care interventions with the strongest evidence of efficacy are the Addiction Comprehensive Health Enhancement Support System (ACHESS) app and Telephone Monitoring and Counseling (TMC). Positive effects for these intervention on drinking outcomes in patients with alcohol use disorders were replicated in a recent randomized controlled study.

sec.title: Summary
sec.p: Continuing care is widely believed to be an important component of treatment for substance use disorders, especially for sustaining positive outcomes. However, many individuals do not attend clinic-based continuing care, due to a variety of reasons including competing work and family responsibilities, disabilities, transportation challenges, and recently the COVID 19 pandemic. Remote continuing care, provided via smartphone apps, text messaging, and various telephonic approaches, has been shown to be effective, and could be used to provide continuing care to patients who would otherwise not receive it. Further work is needed to determine how to effectively combine more traditional continuing care with newer digitized and telephonic approaches.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Because rates of relapse following treatment are high for individuals with substance use disorders, most patients are referred to continuing care to support sustained recovery. Randomized studies have found that patients provided with continuing care have better substance use outcomes than those who are not given continuing care, although the magnitude of these positive effects was relatively small [1]. There is also some suggestion that continuing care interventions provided for longer periods of time and those that involve more active outreach to patients may be more effective [2]. However, it can be difficult to retain patients in traditional clinic-based continuing care, as many need to return to work or family responsibilities following a more intensive episode of treatment.

Digital interventions that do not require clinic attendance and can be provided remotely may be particularly helpful to patients who want continuing care but cannot attend sessions at a clinic or office. The technology could also be used in conjunction with other behavioral interventions to provide automated support in between therapy sessions and to convey information on a patient’s status back to the therapist. This article reviews evidence for the effectiveness of continuing care interventions delivered remotely via smartphone apps, texting, interactive voice response (IVR), and structured telephone counseling.

sec.title: Smartphone Apps
sec.p: There is one smartphone app that was developed specifically for the continuing care phase of treatment, the Addiction Comprehensive Health Enhancement Support System (ACHESS) [3]. It should be noted that ACHESS does not provide a digitized version of an evidence-based substance use disorder treatment, such as cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), through the presentation of sequential modules. Rather, ACHESS has a menu of functions that can be accessed in any order at any time, including links to social supports, information on handling stressful situations, aids for relaxation, self-monitoring, and GPS-linked tools (see Table 1).

In the first large, controlled trial of ACHESS, patients with moderate to severe alcohol use disorder (N=349) who had completed residential treatment were randomized to receive ACHESS and a smartphone with a data plan for 8 months or to a control condition, which consisted of referral to whatever continuing care was available to the patients in their communities [3]. Continuing care services obtained by participants in the control condition were not assessed. Although frequency of reported alcohol use was low in both conditions, patients receiving ACHESS reported 49% fewer risky drinking days in the prior 30 days at 4, 8, and 12 month follow-ups, compared to those in standard care. Rates of alcohol abstinence within the prior 30 days were higher in ACHESS than in standard care at the 8 (78% vs. 67%) and 12 (79% vs. 66%) month follow-ups [3]. A secondary analysis from this study found that ACHESS increased rates of participation in outpatient addiction treatment during follow-up relative to standard care, which partially mediated or accounted for the positive effect of ACHESS on number of risky drinking days [4].

A second controlled trial focused on patients with moderate to severe alcohol use disorder attending intensive outpatient programs (IOP). Patients randomized to receive ACHESS and a smartphone with a data plan for 12 months in addition to standard care (N=68) had fewer heavy drinking days during that 12-month period than those receiving IOP and standard continuing care without ACHESS (N=65) (6.80% days vs. 10.29% days, d= .31, p= .031). [5]. In this study, standard continuing care consisted of weekly outpatient group counseling at the clinic. However, the positive effects of ACHESS did not persist at an 18- month follow-up, and the intervention did not reduce other drug use.

There is some evidence that patients continue to use ACHESS over long periods. For example, participants logged on to ACHESS on an average of 20 days per month in the first two months and about 14 days per month in months 3–12 in the second trial described above [5]. In a third study that examined the implementation of a version of ACHESS for primary care (“Seva”) in three federally qualified health centers, 50–60% of patients given Seva and a smartphone with a data plan were still using the app at least weekly at the end of the 12-month trial [6]. However, these measures of daily or weekly use do not provide information on length of time spent on the app or on functions that were accessed and used.

Several other apps for substance use disorder treatment have been developed and have generated evidence of efficacy, including Dynamicare, ReSET, Computer Based Training for Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT4CBT), and Step Away. However, none of these apps was specifically designed as a continuing care intervention, and none has been evaluated as such. However, it is quite possible that these apps could provide effective continuing care, as they address issues related to relapse prevention, such as identifying risky situations, improving coping behaviors, and increasing involvement in pro-recovery activities.

A web-based relapse prevention intervention was tested in patients who met DSM IV or V criteria for a substance use disorder who were recruited from psychiatric hospitals in Japan. The most common primary drug of choice was methamphetamine (50% of the sample). The intervention, the e-learning Serigaya Methamphetamine Relapse Prevention Program (e-SMARPP) [7] was based on the Matrix Model [8] and consisted of six sessions delivered via five modules that focused on self-monitoring and relapse prevention and included videos and homework. This intervention was compared to a web-based intervention that included self-monitoring but not relapse prevention. The e-SMARPP condition did not differ from the control condition on the primary outcome, longest period of days abstinent from the primary drug [7].

sec.title: Text Messaging Interventions
sec.p: Effective remote continuing care can also be provided by texting interventions. The “Educating and Supporting inQuisitive Youth in Recovery” (ESQYIR) is a 12-week continuing care texting intervention for adolescents with substance use disorders [8]. The intervention consists of twice daily disease management text messages, which include monitoring, feedback, reminders, education, and support. In a study of 80 adolescents who had completed an initial phase of treatment, those randomized to ESQYIR were less likely to have relapsed to use of their primary substance, as indicated by either urine drug screen or self-report, than those randomized to standard care at the 6-month (28.6% vs. 54.1%; OR= 0.72, p= .02) and 9-month (14.7% vs. 42.9%; OR= 0.74, p= .01) follow-ups. They also reported significantly higher self-efficacy (OR= 1.36, p= .03) and were more likely to participate in recovery-oriented activities (6 months: 15 vs. 11 days; 9 months: 18 vs. 13 days; p< .001) [9]. Subsequent mediation analyses shed light on what accounted for the positive effect of ESQYIR on substance use outcomes. The mobile aftercare intervention promoted increased engagement in recovery-oriented behaviors such as participation in extracurricular activities, which mediated the positive effect of the intervention on substance use outcomes [10].

A text messaging continuing care intervention that consisted of monitoring of self-selected drinking goals, motivational text messages, and telephone calls when participants failed to achieve goals or asked for support was evaluated in a randomized study in Switzerland with patients who had entered outpatient treatment for alcohol use problems [11]. The participants in the SMS condition responded to 88% of the SMS prompts, and 44% sent at least one request for help. However, the intervention was not superior to the standard continuing care comparison condition on the primary outcome, percentage of participants who had any at risk drinking during follow-up [11].

sec.title: Interactive Voice Response
sec.p: Rose and colleagues developed an automated telephone continuing care intervention that is delivered via Interactive Voice Response (IVR) [12]. Participants call into the system once per day and report on several factors, including substance use, mood states, craving, self-efficacy, risk situations, sobriety support, substance free recreation, and coping. If a participant is categorized as “at risk” by this assessment, tailored automated feedback is provided. Other features include encouragement to use CBT skills learned in the initial phase of treatment, review of coping skills, and coping skills practice. Each month, participants also receive a personalized voice message from their therapist, which includes comments on progress and suggestions.

The system was evaluated in a study in which individuals with alcohol use disorder who completed 12 weeks of outpatient CBT were randomized to four months of the IVR system or usual care and followed for four months. Any services that were provided in the usual care condition were not described. Most of primary analyses indicated no differences in drinking outcomes between the two conditions. However, a significant group × time interaction (p= .04) on drinking days per week during the 4-month treatment period favored the IVR condition. In addition, in participants who were abstinent at the end of the 12-week initial CBT intervention, outcomes on any drinking at the 2- and 4-month follow-ups and any heavy drinking at the 4-month follow-up favored IVR over usual care [12]. However, there were no treatment effects at the 12-month follow-up.

sec.title: Telephone Continuing Care
sec.p: McKay and colleagues developed Telephone Monitoring and Counseling (TMC), a structured continuing care intervention that can be delivered over the telephone or through a combination of telephone and clinic visits [13]. TMC combines measurement-based care with CBT components, such as identification of high-risk situations and strengthening of coping responses, which are delivered in 15–30 minute individual sessions. Information on concerning changes in risk and protective factors gathered in a brief assessment at the start of each session is used to select the focus or target of the remainder of the session. Versions of TMC ranging from 12 weeks up to 24 months have been evaluated in controlled trials. Prior research has found that when provided to patients with alcohol use disorder who had completed 4–6 weeks of an intensive outpatient program (IOP), TMC improved alcohol use outcomes relative to IOP only [14,15].

Results with patients who have cocaine use disorder have been more mixed. One study found that TMC produced lower rates of cocaine positive urines over 24 months than standard care and CBT comparison conditions [14], while another study found positive effects for TMC on drug use outcomes only in patients who continued to use cocaine or alcohol early in IOP [16]. Wimberly et al. [17] found that patients with cocaine use disorder randomized to TMC had fewer criminal convictions over a 4-year follow-up period than patients randomized to standard care. Conversely, a fourth study found that a more intensive version of TMC that was initiated at the start of IOP produced worse cocaine urine toxicology outcomes than IOP only [18]. Other findings have shown that TMC tends to produce larger effects for poorer prognosis patients and for women [19,20], and that is cost-effective and cost-beneficial [21].

A 12-week version of TMC was evaluated in two recent studies conducted by Timko and colleagues. One study randomized 406 patients receiving inpatient care for co-occurring substance use disorders and mental health disorders to usual care or usual care plus 12 weeks of TMC [22]. Outcomes were measures of substance use, psychological problems, outpatient substance use treatment, and 12-step group involvement over a 15-month follow-up. Both TMC and usual care conditions improved over the follow-up, but there were no significant differences between the conditions. In the second study [23], 298 patients undergoing inpatient detoxification were randomized to usual care or usual care plus 12 weeks of TMC. The primary outcomes were rates of readmission to inpatient detoxification at 3 and 6 months. Results indicated that TMC produced a lower rate of readmission than usual care at 3 months (24.3% vs. 38.6%, p= .014), but not at 6 months (26.0% vs. 33.3%, p= .20).

A recent study evaluated the impact of adding TMC, ACHESS, and a package that integrated both TMC and ACHESS to IOP for alcohol use disorder [5]. All patients received IOP with any continuing care counseling groups they chose to attend at the clinics, and were randomized to also receive TMC, ACHESS, TMC+ACHESS, or no additional continuing care. The primary outcome in this study was percentage of heavy drinking days (PDHD; days with ≥ 4 and ≥ 5 drinks per day for women and men, respectively) during the 12 months in which the interventions were provided. The validity of self-reports of PDHD were supported by a biological measure of recent heavy alcohol use, %dCDT. Secondary outcomes were measures of any drinking, any drug use, negative consequences of drinking, and quality of life. The results pertaining to ACHESS are described above.

In analyses of the impact of adding TMC and TMC+ACHESS to IOP, mean PDHD in months 1–12 was 5.41 in TMC, 5.99 in TMC+ACHESS, and 10.29 in IOP with standard continuing care only. PDHD was lower in TMC (d=.35, p=0.018) and TMC+ACHESS (d= .36, p=0.009) than in IOP with standard continuing care only. Differences in PDHD between TMC+ACHESS, TMC, and ACHESS were small (d≤ .06) and non-significant. A significant effect was also obtained on Any Drinking, which was higher in months 1–12 in IOP with standard continuing care only than in TMC (odds ratio (OR)= 3.02, p=0.01) and TMC+ACHESS (OR= 2.43, p=0.03). There were no treatment group differences on the other secondary measures during months 1–12, and no differences on primary or secondary outcomes at 18 months [5].

Therefore, the results of this study replicate prior studies that found TMC and ACHESS to be efficacious continuing care interventions for patients with alcohol use disorders [3, 14,15]. However, there were two notable additional findings in this new study. First, contrary to study hypotheses, the combination of the two interventions (i.e., TMC+ACHESS) did not achieve better substance use outcomes than either intervention alone. During the 12-month treatment phase, outcomes in TMC, ACHESS, and TMC+ACHES were very similar. Therefore, it appears that the expected additive or synergistic effects of combining the two interventions did not materialize. Second, none of the interventions was significantly better than IOP with standard continuing care at 18 months, which was six months after the mobile interventions had ended. Because the positive effects from continuing care provided remotely via smartphone apps or telephone may deteriorate after the interventions have ended, it may be necessary to extend these interventions to better support long-term recovery. Finally, it is possible that TMC may be more effective if it is delivered with a video component, as in telemedicine, rather than in an audio-only format. However, this has not been tested in a controlled study.

Participation in telephone continuing care has varied considerably across studies. In studies of ETM, the percentage of possible sessions completed ranged from 25% [15] to 56% [5]. In one study providing $10 incentives for each completed TMC session raised the participation rate from 38% to 67% of possible sessions [16]. However, the increased rate of participation did not improve drug use outcomes [16].

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: There is clear evidence that effective continuing care for substance use disorders can be provided remotely through several modalities, including automated smartphone apps, text messaging, IVR, and structured telephone counseling. Studies of mediation effects suggest that these interventions tend to work by increasing use of addiction treatment [4], engagement in recovery-oriented activities [10], and participation in 12-step programs [24] during follow-up periods. At this point, evidence for the effectiveness of these interventions is stronger for patients with alcohol use disorder than for other drug use disorders. However, this at least in part reflects that fact that most studies of digital and remote continuing care have focused to a greater degree on alcohol use disorder. As more research is conducted, the evidence base for drug use disorders may grow. In particular, there is a need for studies of digital continuing care in the context of medication treatment for opioid use disorder. Furthermore, most studies of smartphone apps for addiction have looked at effects early in treatment, rather than during the continuing care phase. At least some of these apps are likely to also be effective when delivered as continuing care, although this needs to be confirmed by further research.

Despite the initial promise of digitized mobile health interventions, significant challenges remain in the provision of continuing care via mobile health apps and SMS [25]. Users must have access to a smartphone and data plan, or to a telephone with SMS capabilities for texting-based interventions. In order to fully realize the potential of this technology to provide just-in-time interventions during periods of high relapse risk, users of digitized continuing care must be willing to carry the devices with them and engage their functions when needed. Rates of app use are often low and tend to decrease relatively rapidly. Therefore, to maximize the efficacy of digitized continuing care, further work is needed to increase sustained use of apps and other interventions. Finally, additional research is needed to better understand how to integrate therapist delivered counseling and digital recovery aids to maximize the impact of these interventions.

ack.title: Financial Support and Sponsorship
ack.p: Preparation of this article was supported by National Institute on Drug Abuse (NIDA) award 2K24 DA029062, to James R. McKay.

fn.p: James R. McKay one of the principal developers of Telephone Monitoring and Counseling (TMC). However, the intervention is in the public domain and he does not derive any direct financial benefit from it.

ref.mixed-citation: BlodgettJC, MaiselNC, FuhIL, How effective is continuing care for substance use disorders? A meta-analytic review. Journal of Substance Abuse Treatment 2014; 46:87–97.2407579610.1016/j.jsat.2013.08.022PMC3840113

GustafsonDH, McTavishFM, ChihM-Y, A smartphone application to support recovery from alcoholism: A randomized controlled trial. JAMA Psychiatry 2014; 71:566–572.2467116510.1001/jamapsychiatry.2013.4642PMC4016167

GlassJE, McKayJR, GustafsonDH, Treatment seeking as a mechanism of change in a randomized controlled trial of a mobile health intervention to support recovery from alcohol use disorders. Journal of Substance Abuse Treatment 2017; 77:57–66.2847627310.1016/j.jsat.2017.03.011PMC5880274

McKayJR, GustafsonDH, IveyM, Efficacy and comparative effectiveness of telephone and smartphone remote continuing care interventions for alcohol use disorder: A randomized controlled trial. Addiction 2021; e-publication ahead of print. DOI: 10.1111/add.15771PMC1060097734859519

note.p: **This was the first study to compare three remotely delivered continuing care interventions (e.g., TMC, ACHESS, and TMC+ACHESS) provided in addition to intensive outpatient programs (IOP) for alcohol use disorder to each other and to IOP only. The importance of being able to provide effective continuing care remotely has been made clear by the COVID 19 pandemic. Results indicated that all three interventions produced lower PDHD than IOP only during the 12 months in which they were provided.

ref.mixed-citation: QuanbeckA, GustafsonDH, MarschLA, Implementing a mobile health system to integrate the treatment of addiction into primary care: A hybrid implementation-effectiveness study. Journal of Medical Internet Research 2018; 20:e37.2938262410.2196/jmir.8928PMC5811649

TakanoA, MiyamotoY, ShinozakiT, Effect of a web-based relapse prevention program on abstinence among Japanese drug users: A pilot randomized controlled trial. Journal of Substance Abuse Treatment 2020; 111:37–46.3208783710.1016/j.jsat.2019.12.001

note.p: * This study was the first to evaluate the web-based Serigaya Methamphetamine Relapse Prevention Program (e-SMARPP) in comparison to another web-based intervention that included self-monitoring but not relapse prevention.

ref.mixed-citation: RawsonR, ShoptawSJ, ObertJR, An intensive outpatient approach for cocaine abuse treatment: The Matrix Model. Journal of Substance Abuse Treatment 1995; 12:117–127.762338910.1016/0740-5472(94)00080-b

GonzalesR, HernandezM, MurphyDA, AngA. Youth recovery outcomes at 6 and 9 months following participation in a mobile texting recovery support aftercare pilot study. American Journal of Addictions 2016; 25:62–68.10.1111/ajad.12322PMC496653226689171

note.p: *This was the first study to identify a therapeutic mechanism of action in an efficacious texting continuing care intervention for adolescents. The results indicated that the positive effect of the intervention was partially accounted for by increases in recovery-oriented behavior produced by the intervention.

ref.mixed-citation: HuagS, LuchtMJ, JohnU, A pilot study on the feasibility and acceptability of a text message-based aftercare treatment programme among alcohol outpatients. Alcohol and Alcoholism 2015; 50:188–194.2560024910.1093/alcalc/agu107

note.p: *The study was one of the first to examine the impact on substance use and mental health outcomes of a brief (i.e., 12 week) version of Telephone Monitoring and Counseling (TMC) with dual diagnosis patients.

ref.mixed-citation: TimkoC, BelowM, VittorioL, Randomized controlled trial of enhanced telephone monitoring with detoxification patients: 3- and 6-month outcomes. Journal of Substance Abuse Treatment 2019; 99:24–31.3079739110.1016/j.jsat.2018.12.008PMC6568005

note.p: * The study was the first to examine the impact on readmission rates of a brief (i.e., 12 week) version of Telephone Monitoring and Counseling (TMC) with dual diagnosis patients.

ref.mixed-citation: MensingerJL, LynchKG, TenHaveTR, McKayJR. Mediators of telephone-based continuing care for alcohol and cocaine dependence. Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology 2007; 75:775–784.1790785910.1037/0022-006X.75.5.775PMC2677065

NesvagS, McKayJR. Digital interventions to support people in recovery from substance use disorders: A review of feasibility and effects. Journal of Medical Internet Research 2018; 20, e255. doi: 10.2196/jmir.987330139724PMC6127498

list-item.p: Chat room proving connections to other ACHESS users
Connections to social supports via telephone and the internet
Library of materials on how to handle risky situations and other stressors
Relaxation aids, including links to music
Daily and weekly assessments to measure progress
Capability to send assessment results to patients and therapists
GPS-based function that is activated when patients are near locations where they have used alcohol or drugs frequently
To increase rates of sustained recovery from substance use disorders, effective approaches to providing continuing care remotely are needed.

Digital devices including smartphone apps hold considerable promise for the delivery of continuing care to individuals with substance use disorders, especially for those who are not able to attend clinic-based care.

Recent research indicates that the Addiction Comprehensive Health Enhancement Support System (ACHESS) app and Telephone Monitoring and Counseling (TMC) provide effective continuing care for individuals with alcohol use disorder.
Further work is needed to determine how best to effectively combine traditional continuing care with newer digitized and telephonic approaches.
